nativeRange.habitat: Shallow ponds, lake margins, marshes, ditches, slow streams, and seasonally flooded wetlands across temperate Eurasia — historically common in the Upper Rhine and other nutrient-poor water bodies, and persistent in rice paddies and irrigation channels in its East Asian range.

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich aquasoil or fine sand/gravel over root tabs
open foreground or midground with direct overhead light
gentle, steady flow over the rhizome line
optional pressurised CO2 for faster, denser carpeting
minimum substrate depth of roughly 2–5 cm to anchor runners

avoidWith: large boisterous fish that uproot foreground plants
vigorously burrowing loaches and large catfish

summary: Four-Leaf Clover (Marsilea quadrifolia) is a temperate-hardy aquatic fern from Europe and Asia, valued for its distinctive four-leaflet shape and forgiving nature. It carpets the foreground under low to medium light without requiring CO2, making it one of the more accessible carpeting plants for beginners.
careNotes: Four-Leaf Clover is an aquatic fern (family Marsileaceae, order Salviniales) and is the temperate-Eurasian counterpart to the Australian Marsilea hirsuta (fw-plant-075) and the Southeast Asian Marsilea crenata (fw-plant-037). Native to central and southern Europe, Caucasia, western Siberia, Afghanistan, south-west India, China, Japan, and Vietnam, it grows in shallow ponds, lake margins, marshes, ditches, and seasonally flooded wetlands; it was historically common in the Upper Rhine in nutrient-poor waters and remains a familiar plant of East Asian rice paddies. Like its relatives, the species shows strong phenotypic plasticity — meaning its appearance changes significantly with growing conditions. Emersed plants (those growing above the waterline) and freshly converted specimens reliably produce the iconic four-leaflet clover shape that gives the plant its common name and makes it the most consistent four-leaflet form in the genus. Submerged plants may produce stems carrying one to four leaflets, with leaflet count, petiole length, and overall height all shifting in response to light, CO2, and nutrient levels. Submerged height typically ranges from about 3–15 cm, making it taller and more upright than M. crenata or M. hirsuta; the plant spreads horizontally via a creeping rhizome (an underground stem that sends up new fronds as it travels) to form a low foreground carpet or a slightly taller midground patch when light is reduced. The species is notably temperature-tolerant, with reported cultivation tolerance from roughly 4 to 30 °C; for submerged aquarium use, 18–28 °C is the generally recommended range. pH preference is around 6.0–7.5, with soft to moderately hard water (gH approximately 2–15). A nutrient-rich substrate such as aquasoil is generally advised, or an inert substrate supplemented with root tabs, since the plant draws most of its nutrition through its roots rather than the water column. Medium light is sufficient for a dense, compact carpet; the plant tolerates low light but will respond with taller, leggier, slower growth rather than the flat carpeting form most aquascapers seek. CO2 injection is not required, and the plant will carpet without it, but pressurised CO2 noticeably tightens leaflet spacing and speeds coverage. Growth rate is generally slow to moderate. To plant, tuck small rhizome clumps with their roots into the substrate every few centimetres; established mats can be trimmed and divided to fill additional areas. Conservation/regulatory note: M. quadrifolia is listed as Least Concern globally by the IUCN but is protected or considered threatened in several individual European countries, while in North America it is naturalised and treated as a weed in parts of the northeastern United States — keepers should avoid releasing trimmings into local waterways.
breedingNotes: Aquarium propagation is vegetative: the rhizome creeps horizontally as a runner and sends up new fronds at intervals, gradually expanding the carpet on its own. Aquascapers can accelerate coverage by lifting and dividing established clumps, or by cutting lateral shoots that have their own attached roots and replanting them across the foreground. In the wild and in emersed culture, the species also reproduces sexually via sporocarps (the dark brown, ellipsoid, bean-like spore cases approximately 5 mm long that are characteristic of the Marsileaceae family), but sporocarp reproduction is essentially never used in submerged aquarium conditions.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied primary. Confirmed Four-Leaf Clover common name, carpeting foreground placement, moderate-to-high light preference, CO2 not required but highly beneficial for faster/tighter carpeting, moderate growth rate with nutrient-rich substrate and CO2, temperature range ~20–29 °C (68–84 °F), runner-based propagation, and easy/beginner-friendly care rating. Page mixes M. quadrifolia and M. crenata in title/body text (a known retail conflation across the dwarf Marsilea group), so taxonomy and native range were taken from Wikipedia rather than this source.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Marsileaceae, order Salviniales, division Polypodiophyta), common names 'four-leaf clover' and 'European waterclover', native range across central/southern Europe, Caucasia, western Siberia, Afghanistan, south-west India, China, Japan, and Vietnam, characteristic four-parted leaf with ~3/4 in leaflets and ~8 in petioles, sporocarp reproduction (dark brown ellipsoid ~3/16 in spore cases), IUCN Least Concern / NatureServe Secure conservation status, and naturalised/weed status in the northeastern United States for over a century.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Cross-checked extreme temperature tolerance (~4–30 °C in cultivation), native distribution 'Europe, temperate Asia; introduced in North America', historic Upper Rhine occurrence in nutrient-poor waters, dual submerged/emersed growth, pond-suitability, and propagation by runners, division, and removal of daughter plants. Database entry warns that material sold under the M. quadrifolia name in the trade is sometimes misidentified — identification requires emersed forms and sporocarps.
